text: Fill in the blanks about patterns of inheritance
questions: In autosomal dominant inheritance, an affected heterozygous parent has a *50/fifty* percent chance of passing the mutant allele to each offspring, and the trait shows *vertical/vertical transmission* transmission through every generation.

Autosomal recessive disorders require *two/2* copies of the mutant allele for phenotypic expression, and carrier parents who are both *heterozygous/heterozygotes* have a 25% risk of having an affected child.
X-linked recessive disorders predominantly affect *males/male* because they have only *one/single* X chromosome and cannot be carriers.

The phenomenon where a genetic disease becomes more severe in successive generations is called *anticipation/genetic anticipation*, classically seen in trinucleotide *repeat/triplet repeat* expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy.
